text: First is identification of the proximal and distal ends of the abnormal segment.
Hold the mesentery perpendicular to the instrument to make a mesenteric window for the site of bowel transection.
The small bowel is divided with a 2.5 mm linear stapler passed through the mesenteric window.
The mesentery of the specimen is divided with an electrosurgical device.
A traction suture is placed at the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel to align the two portions of the bowel prior to re-anastomosis.
If appropriately placed, this stitch can also provide support at the end of the anastomotic staple line.
Hook electrocautery is used to create openings in the bowel lumen.
Care not to injure the back wall of the bowel during this step.
A 60 millimeter long, 2.5 millimeter linear stapler is used to reestablish intestinal continuity.
Pay careful attention to have the stapler placed fully within the bowel lumen.
It is important to also ensure the bowel is aligned evenly.

If a large anastomosis is desired, a double stapled anastomosis can be performed with rotating the anastomosis 180 degrees to accommodate another 60 or 45 millimeter long, 2.5 millimeter linear stapler.
The stapled suture line is inspected for bleeding, which if present is controlled with interrupted sutures.
Then a proximal stitch is applied.
The common enterotomy can be closed in many techniques.
Sutures or staples may be used.
A target suture can be placed as a handle to allow for more dexterity of the left hand without handling the tissue more than necessary.
A running stitch is employed to close the remainder of the enterotomy.
A single layer closure with absorbable suture, such as a 2-0 polysorb, is usually adequate, cut from 20 to 30 centimeters.
For stapled closures, sutures can be utilized to ensure the enterotomy is completely resected.
The stapled closure is most commonly performed in a Heinecke-Mekulitz orientation or where the enterotomy is closed in a transversal fashion.
Shown here is the completed suture line along with the completed staple line.
For the final step, place a stay suture at the end of the mesenteric defect.
Then, start a running suture to close the remainder of the defect, ending your closure at the previously placed stay suture.
This concludes the training video.
